places.name: Sychar
places.description: A Samaritan town near the plot of ground Jacob gave to his son Joseph. Identified with Askar, on the eastern slope of Mount Ebal, near Jacob's Well.

places.name: Jacob's Well
places.description: A well dug by the patriarch Jacob at the foot of Mount Gerizim, approximately 32 meters deep. Continuously venerated since the 4th century AD. Now located within an Eastern Orthodox church at Tell Balata.

places.name: Mount Gerizim
places.description: The sacred mountain of the Samaritans, where they built their rival temple to Jerusalem's. The woman at the well referred to this mountain in John 4:20: “Our ancestors worshiped on this mountain.”

places.name: Shechem
places.description: Ancient Canaanite city at the base of Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. Abraham camped here (Gen 12:6). Jacob bought land here (Gen 33:19). Identified with Tell Balata in modern Nablus.

places.name: Jerusalem
places.description: The holy city, capital of Judah, site of the Temple and of Jesus’ passion, death, and resurrection. Center of Jewish religious life in the first century.

places.name: Temple Mount
places.description: The platform on which Herod’s Temple stood. The largest religious enclosure in the ancient world, measuring roughly 36 acres. Destroyed by Rome in 70 AD.

places.name: Bethlehem
places.description: City of David, birthplace of Jesus (Matt 2:1, Luke 2:4–7). Located about 10 km south of Jerusalem in the Judean hills.

places.name: Nazareth
places.description: The hometown of Jesus and Mary and Joseph (Luke 2:39, 4:16). A small Galilean village in the first century, now a city of over 70,000. Jesus was rejected here (Luke 4:28–30).

places.name: Capernaum
places.description: Jesus’ base of ministry in Galilee (Matt 4:13). On the northwestern shore of the Sea of Galilee. Site of the healing of the centurion’s servant and Peter’s home. Ruins include a 4th-century synagogue.

places.name: Sea of Galilee
places.description: A freshwater lake in northern Israel, also called Lake Kinneret. 21 km long, 13 km wide. Site of Jesus’ calming the storm, walking on water, and calling the first disciples.

places.name: Bethsaida
places.description: Hometown of Philip, Andrew, and Peter (John 1:44). Located near the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee where the Jordan flows in. Site of the healing of a blind man (Mark 8:22–26).

places.name: Cana
places.description: Site of Jesus’ first sign — turning water into wine at a wedding feast (John 2:1–11). Located in the hills of Galilee. Also where Jesus healed the royal official’s son from a distance (John 4:46–54).

places.name: Bethany
places.description: Village on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, home of Lazarus, Mary, and Martha (John 11:1). Site of the raising of Lazarus. Jesus frequently stayed here during his final week.

places.name: Jericho
places.description: One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. Located in the Jordan Valley, 258 m below sea level. Site of the healing of blind Bartimaeus and the conversion of Zacchaeus (Luke 19).

places.name: Mount of Olives
places.description: A ridge east of Jerusalem overlooking the Temple Mount. Site of Gethsemane, the Ascension, and Jesus’ triumphal entry. Jewish tradition holds that the resurrection will begin here.

places.name: Gethsemane
places.description: A garden at the foot of the Mount of Olives where Jesus prayed before his arrest (Matt 26:36–46, John 18:1–11). Ancient olive trees still grow on the site today.

places.name: Pool of Siloam
places.description: A ritual pool in the City of David, fed by Hezekiah’s Tunnel. Jesus sent the man born blind to wash here (John 9:7). Excavations in 2004 confirmed its first-century use.

places.name: Jordan River
places.description: The river forming the eastern boundary of Canaan. John baptized here (Matt 3:13–17). The traditional baptism site of Jesus, called Qasr el-Yahud, is maintained by the Israeli government.

places.name: Caesarea Philippi
places.description: A city at the foot of Mount Hermon, near the source of the Jordan River. Site of Peter’s confession: “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God” (Matt 16:13–16).

places.name: Emmaus
places.description: A village about 11 km from Jerusalem. Site of the appearance of the risen Jesus to two disciples who did not recognize him until he broke bread with them (Luke 24:13–35).

places.name: Mount Sinai
places.description: The mountain where God gave the Law to Moses (Exod 19–20). Identified with Jebel Musa in the Sinai Peninsula. Also called Horeb. Site of Elijah’s encounter with God (1 Kings 19:8–18).

places.name: Hebron
places.description: One of the oldest cities in Canaan. Abraham settled here (Gen 13:18) and purchased the Cave of Machpelah as a burial site for Sarah. David was crowned king here before moving to Jerusalem.

places.name: Beersheba
places.description: The southernmost city of ancient Israel, marking the border of the settled land (“from Dan to Beersheba”). Abraham dug a well here (Gen 21:30–31). Site of Isaac’s altar and Jacob’s vision.

places.name: Mount Carmel
places.description: A coastal mountain range in northern Israel. Site of Elijah’s dramatic contest with the 450 prophets of Baal and the 400 prophets of Asherah (1 Kings 18:19–40).

places.name: Mount Tabor
places.description: A dome-shaped mountain in the Jezreel Valley, rising 575 m above sea level. Traditionally identified as the site of the Transfiguration (Matt 17:1–8). Site of Deborah and Barak’s victory (Judg 4–5).

places.name: Shiloh
places.description: The location of the Tabernacle for most of the period of the Judges (Josh 18:1). Here Hannah prayed for a son and Samuel was dedicated to the Lord (1 Sam 1–3). Destroyed by the Philistines c. 1050 BC.

places.name: Megiddo
places.description: A strategic fortress city guarding the Jezreel Valley pass. Site of many decisive battles through history. Its name gives us “Armageddon” (Har Megiddo, “Mount of Megiddo”, Rev 16:16).

places.name: Samaria (city)
places.description: Capital of the northern kingdom of Israel, built by Omri (1 Kings 16:24). Fell to the Assyrians in 722 BC (2 Kings 17). Rebuilt by Herod the Great and renamed Sebaste. Philip preached here (Acts 8:5).

places.name: Caesarea Maritima
places.description: A major port city built by Herod the Great. Roman administrative capital of Judea. Cornelius was here when Peter came (Acts 10). Paul was imprisoned here for two years (Acts 23–26).

places.name: Antioch (Syria)
places.description: Third largest city of the Roman Empire. First place where followers of Jesus were called “Christians” (Acts 11:26). Base of Paul’s three missionary journeys. Site of the confrontation between Paul and Peter (Gal 2:11).
